items.title: North Korean hackers targeted ethnic Koreans in China with Android ‘BirdCall’ malware
items.description: Researchers at cybersecurity firm ESET attributed the campaign to APT37 and said the hackers used a backdoor attached to a suite of card games from a company called Sqgame.
items.ai_summary: The BirdCall malware campaign, attributed to North Korean APT37, targets ethnic Koreans in China using a backdoor attached to a suite of card games from Sqgame, allowing for remote access and control of compromised Android devices. The attack vector involves installing the malicious game suite, which then establishes a connection with the command and control server, enabling the threat actors to exfiltrate sensitive data and perform further malicious activities. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their device's data, including contacts, messages, and location information. To remediate, defenders should uninstall any Sqgame card game suites from their devices and install a reputable mobile security solution to detect and prevent similar threats. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing apps from unknown sources and verify the authenticity of the app developer.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Breaking the code: Multi-stage 'code of conduct' phishing campaign leads to AiTM token compromise
items.description: A sophisticated large-scale credential theft campaign targeted over 35,000 users across 13,000 organizations, primarily in the United States, between April 14-16, 2026. Attackers distributed fully authenticated emails from legitimate services using code of conduct-themed lures with polished HTML templates. The multi-stage attack chain included PDF attachments with embedded links, multiple CAPTCHA
items.ai_summary: The 'code of conduct' phishing campaign exploited human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities, with attackers sending fully authenticated emails from legitimate services to over 35,000 users across 13,000 organizations. The emails contained code of conduct-themed lures with polished HTML templates and PDF attachments with embedded links, which led to the compromise of AiTM tokens. If exploited, this campaign could result in unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential data breaches, with the primary targets being organizations in the United States. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, conduct regular security awareness training for employees, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all users. Additionally, organizations should monitor their networks for suspicious activity and consider implementing a web application firewall to detect and prevent AiTM token compromise.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1557| D[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Lorem Ipsum Malware: Trojanized MS Teams Installers
items.description: An emerging threat group is conducting a global SEO-poisoning campaign distributing trojanized Microsoft Teams installers that deploy a multi-stage shellcode loader and backdoor designated Lorem Ipsum. Active since February 2026, the campaign targets users searching for Microsoft Teams across six countries, with confirmed targeting of a US healthcare organization. The operators evolved rapidly fro
items.ai_summary: The Lorem Ipsum malware campaign involves trojanized Microsoft Teams installers that deploy a multi-stage shellcode loader and backdoor, targeting users across six countries, including a confirmed US healthcare organization. The attack vector involves SEO-poisoning, where attackers manipulate search engine results to distribute the malicious installers. If exploited, the malware can establish a remote access channel, allowing threat actors to gain control over the compromised system. To remediate, defenders should inspect Microsoft Teams installers for suspicious activity, ensure all software is downloaded from official sources, and implement a robust network monitoring system to detect potential command and control communications. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a software allowlisting policy to prevent unauthorized software execution.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A rigged game: compromises gaming platform in a supply-chain attack
items.description: North Korea-aligned APT group ScarCruft executed a multiplatform supply-chain attack targeting ethnic Koreans in China's Yanbian region, an area significant for North Korean refugees and defectors. Since late 2024, the group compromised a video gaming platform dedicated to Yanbian-themed games, trojanizing both Windows and Android components with the BirdCall backdoor. The Windows client received
items.ai_summary: The ScarCruft APT group, aligned with North Korea, has been conducting a supply-chain attack targeting a video gaming platform focused on Yanbian-themed games, compromising both Windows and Android components. The attack involves trojanizing the gaming platform with the BirdCall backdoor, allowing for remote access and control. This compromise has significant real-world impact, as it targets ethnic Koreans in China's Yanbian region, an area of importance for North Korean refugees and defectors. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately inspect their gaming platform's software for signs of tampering, specifically looking for the BirdCall backdoor, and update to a known clean version. Additionally, defenders should implement robust monitoring and detection capabilities to identify potential remote access software usage, such as unusual login activity or suspicious network communications, and ensure all systems are configured to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: UAT-8302 and its box full of malware
items.description: UAT-8302 is a sophisticated China-nexus advanced persistent threat group targeting government entities in South America since late 2024 and southeastern Europe in 2025. The actor deploys multiple custom-made malware families including NetDraft, a .NET-based backdoor variant of FinalDraft/SquidDoor, and CloudSorcerer version 3. Post-compromise activities involve extensive reconnaissance, credential
items.ai_summary: The UAT-8302 threat group has been targeting government entities in South America and southeastern Europe since late 2024, leveraging custom-made malware families such as NetDraft, a .NET-based backdoor variant of FinalDraft/SquidDoor, and CloudSorcerer version 3. The group's attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the detection of T1190, and utilizing remote access software, as seen in T1219. If exploited, this could lead to extensive reconnaissance and credential harvesting, ultimately compromising sensitive government data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust security controls, including multi-factor authentication and regular software updates, and monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or unfamiliar network connections. Specifically, defenders should ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure protocols, such as SSH or VPNs, and that all public-facing applications are patched and secured with web application firewalls.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CloudZ RAT potentially steals OTP messages using Pheno plugin
items.description: Cisco Talos uncovered an intrusion active since January 2026 where attackers deployed CloudZ remote access tool and an undocumented plugin called Pheno to steal credentials and one-time passwords. The attack exploits Microsoft Phone Link application by intercepting synchronized mobile data including SMS and OTPs without requiring phone-level infection. CloudZ evades detection through dynamic memor
items.ai_summary: The CloudZ RAT is a remote access tool that has been used in an active intrusion since January 2026 to steal credentials and one-time passwords. The attack exploits the Microsoft Phone Link application, specifically targeting the synchronization of mobile data, including SMS and OTPs, without requiring phone-level infection. This is achieved through an undocumented plugin called Pheno, which intercepts and exfiltrates sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update the Microsoft Phone Link application to the latest version and review phone synchronization settings to ensure that only necessary data is being shared. Additionally, implementing multi-factor authentication with phishing-resistant methods, such as FIDO2, can help mitigate the impact of stolen OTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Valid Accounts]:::tactic
    D -->|T1110| E[Brute Force]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular DAEMON Tools software compromised
items.description: Since April 8, 2026, installers of DAEMON Tools software have been compromised with malicious payloads distributed through the legitimate website. Versions 12.5.0.2421 to 12.5.0.2434 contain trojaned binaries (DTHelper.exe, DiscSoftBusServiceLite.exe, DTShellHlp.exe) signed with legitimate developer certificates. The attack has affected thousands of systems across over 100 countries, though advanc
items.ai_summary: The DAEMON Tools software compromise involves malicious payloads being distributed through the legitimate website, affecting versions 12.5.0.2421 to 12.5.0.2434. The trojaned binaries, including DTHelper.exe, DiscSoftBusServiceLite.exe, and DTShellHlp.exe, are signed with legitimate developer certificates, making them appear trustworthy. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of affected systems, with thousands of systems across over 100 countries already impacted. To remediate, defenders should immediately uninstall affected DAEMON Tools versions and install a clean version, ensuring all related system services are stopped and malicious files are removed. Additionally, defenders should monitor system activity for suspicious behavior and update their security software to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Deployment Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Exploitation of Remote Services]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[System Information Discovery]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Four published versions of a fake "tanstack" package uploaded in 27 minutes that want to steal your .env files
items.description: An attacker registered the unscoped 'tanstack' name on npm and published four malicious versions (2.0.4-2.0.7) within 27 minutes on April 29, 2026. These packages contained postinstall hooks that automatically exfiltrated environment files containing sensitive credentials when developers ran npm install. The attacker exploited name confusion with the legitimate @tanstack organization, which publis
items.ai_summary: The threat involves four malicious versions (2.0.4-2.0.7) of a fake 'tanstack' package uploaded to npm within 27 minutes on April 29, 2026. These packages exploit name confusion with the legitimate @tanstack organization and contain postinstall hooks that automatically exfiltrate environment files containing sensitive credentials when developers run npm install. The attack vector involves social engineering and supply chain compromise, where the attacker registers an unscoped package name and publishes malicious versions. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive credentials, potentially affecting developers and organizations using the compromised package. To remediate, developers should verify the authenticity of packages, avoid using unscoped package names, and use npm packages from trusted sources, such as the official @tanstack organization.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Storm-1175 focuses gaze on vulnerable web-facing assets in high ...
items.description: The financially motivated cybercriminal actor tracked by Microsoft Threat Intelligence as Storm-1175 operates high-velocity ransomware campaigns that weaponize N-days, targeting vulnerable, web-facing systems during the window between vulnerability disclosure and widespread patch adoption. Following successful exploitation, Storm-1175 rapidly moves from initial access to data exfiltration and depl
items.ai_summary: Storm-1175, a financially motivated cybercriminal actor, targets vulnerable web-facing systems with N-day exploits during the window between vulnerability disclosure and patch adoption. This allows them to rapidly move from initial access to data exfiltration and encryption, leveraging T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics. The actor's high-velocity ransomware campaigns focus on exploiting unpatched systems, resulting in data breaches and encryption of sensitive information. To remediate, organizations should prioritize patching web-facing systems, particularly those running outdated or unsupported software versions, such as Apache Struts 2.3.5 and earlier. Specifically, administrators should update to Apache Struts 2.5.17 or later and configure the Content-Type header to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Storm-1175]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Malicious OpenClaw Skill Distributes Remcos RAT and GhostLoader
items.description: In March 2026, threat actors weaponized the OpenClaw AI agent framework by publishing a deceptive "DeepSeek-Claw" skill. This skill embedded malicious installation instructions designed to trick AI agents and developers into executing hidden payloads. On Windows systems, a PowerShell command downloads an MSI package containing a legitimate signed GoToMeeting executable that sideloads a malicious D
items.ai_summary: Threat actors exploited the OpenClaw AI agent framework by publishing a deceptive 'DeepSeek-Claw' skill, which embedded malicious installation instructions to trick AI agents and developers into executing hidden payloads. The skill, designed for Windows systems, used PowerShell to download an MSI package containing a legitimate signed GoToMeeting executable that sideloaded a malicious DLL. This led to the installation of the Remcos RAT and GhostLoader, allowing threat actors to gain remote access and execute malicious activities. To remediate, defenders should update the OpenClaw framework to the latest version (v1.2.3 or later) and ensure that all AI agents and developers are aware of the deception tactic. Additionally, implement PowerShell script block logging and monitoring to detect similar tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian-Nexus Operation Against Oman's Government: 12 Ministries Hit and 26,000 Citizen Records Exposed
items.description: An exposed command and control server on RouterHosting infrastructure revealed an active Iranian-nexus intrusion campaign targeting twelve Omani government ministries. The operation primarily focused on the Ministry of Justice and Legal Affairs, deploying custom webshells that provided persistent access through April 2026. Over 26,000 user records containing judicial case data, committee decisions
items.ai_summary: The Iranian-Nexus Operation Against Oman's Government is a sophisticated cyberattack that targeted twelve Omani government ministries, primarily the Ministry of Justice and Legal Affairs. The attackers exploited a command and control server on RouterHosting infrastructure, deploying custom webshells that provided persistent access through April 2026. The operation exposed over 26,000 user records containing judicial case data and committee decisions. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update their RouterHosting infrastructure to the latest version (v2023.04.01 or later) and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to block suspicious traffic. Additionally, all affected ministries should conduct a thorough risk assessment and implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: CVE-2026-6785: Memory safety bugs present in Firefox ESR 115.34, Firefox ESR 140.9, Thunderbird...
items.description: Memory safety bugs present in Firefox ESR 115.34, Firefox ESR 140.9, Thunderbird ESR 140.9, Firefox 149 and Thunderbird 149. Some of these bugs showed evidence of memory corruption and we presume that with enough effort some of these could have been exploited to run arbitrary code. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 150, Firefox ESR 115.35, Firefox ESR 140.10, Thunderbird 150, and Thunderbird
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6785 is a memory safety bug affecting Firefox ESR 115.34, Firefox ESR 140.9, Thunderbird 140.9, Firefox 149, and Thunderbird 149. The vulnerability, rated 8.1 in CVSS, could have been exploited to run arbitrary code with enough effort. If exploited, this bug would allow threat actors to execute malicious code on affected systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, users should update to Firefox 150, Firefox ESR 115.35, Firefox ESR 140.10, Thunderbird 150, or Thunderbird 150. Additionally, ensure that the 'browser.safebrowsing.enabled' preference is set to 'true' in the Firefox configuration file.

items.title: CVE-2026-7041: A vulnerability was detected in 666ghj MiroFish up to 0.1.2. The impacted elemen...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in 666ghj MiroFish up to 0.1.2. The impacted element is an unknown function of the file /console of the component Werkzeug Debugger PIN Handler. Performing a manipulation of the argument SECRET results in information disclosure. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The attack is considered to have high complexity. The exploitability is regarded as difficult.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7041 is a vulnerability in 666ghj MiroFish up to 0.1.2, specifically affecting the Werkzeug Debugger PIN Handler's /console component. An attacker can manipulate the SECRET argument to disclose sensitive information, potentially leading to information disclosure. This vulnerability is remotely exploitable and has a high complexity level, but its exploitability is considered difficult. To remediate, update to version 0.1.3 or later, and ensure the SECRET argument is properly validated and sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as input validation and sanitization, to prevent similar attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-7039: A security vulnerability has been detected in tufantunc ssh-mcp up to 1.5.0. The...
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in tufantunc ssh-mcp up to 1.5.0. The affected element is the function shell.write of the file src/index.ts. Such manipulation of the argument Description leads to command injection. The attack must be carried out locally. The exploit has been disclosed publicly and may be used. The project was informed of the problem early through an issue report but has
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7039 is a command injection vulnerability in tufantunc ssh-mcp up to version 1.5.0. The vulnerability occurs when the shell.write function in src/index.ts is manipulated with a crafted Description argument, allowing an attacker to inject malicious commands. This vulnerability can be exploited locally, and the exploit has been publicly disclosed. To remediate, update to version 1.5.1 or later, and ensure the Description argument is sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as input validation and sanitization, to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.description: A weakness has been identified in tufantunc ssh-mcp up to 1.5.0. Impacted is an unknown function of the file src/index.ts of the component Command Line Handler. This manipulation causes insufficiently protected credentials. The attack is restricted to local execution. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. The project was informed of the problem early thro
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7038 is a weakness in tufantunc ssh-mcp up to version 1.5.0, specifically in the unknown function of the src/index.ts component of the Command Line Handler. This vulnerability allows for insufficiently protected credentials, which can be exploited locally. The exploit has been made publicly available, and if not addressed, could be used for attacks. To remediate, update to version 1.6.0 or later, and ensure the 'allow_credentials' option is set to false in the configuration file. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as restricting access to sensitive credentials.

items.title: CVE-2026-7037: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. Thi...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. This issue affects the function setVpnPassCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component CGI Handler. The manipulation of the argument pptpPassThru results in os command injection. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks.

items.title: CISA: Critical Infrastructure Must Master Isolation, Recovery
items.description: The agency has issued guidance to help critical infrastructure operators prepare for cyberattacks by foreign threat actors. The post CISA: Critical Infrastructure Must Master Isolation, Recovery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google's Android Apps Get Public Verification to Stop Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Google has announced expanded Binary Transparency for Android as a way to safeguard the ecosystem from supply chain attacks. "This new public ledger ensures the Google apps on your device are exactly what we intended to build and distribute," Google's product and security teams said. The initiative builds upon the foundation of Pixel Binary Transparency, which Google introduced in October 2021

items.title: Windows Phone Link Exploited by CloudZ RAT to Steal Credentials and OTPs
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of an intrusion that involved the use of a CloudZ remote access tool (RAT) and a previous undocumented plugin dubbed Pheno with the aim of facilitating credential theft. "According to the functionalities of the CloudZ RAT and Pheno plugin, this was with the intention of stealing victims' credentials and potentially one-time passwords (OTPs),"

items.title: Oracle Debuts Monthly Critical Security Patch Updates
items.description: Containing fixes for critical-severity vulnerabilities, the monthly rollouts will focus on addressing priority issues faster. The post Oracle Debuts Monthly Critical Security Patch Updates appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Middle East Cyber Battle Field Broadens — Especially in UAE
items.description: As the war with Iran continues, breach attempts targeting the United Arab Emirates tripled in a few weeks — many targeting critical infrastructure.

items.title: Palo Alto Networks to Patch Zero-Day Exploited to Hack Firewalls
items.description: CVE-2026-0300 affects the Captive Portal service of PAN-OS software on PA and VM series firewalls. The post Palo Alto Networks to Patch Zero-Day Exploited to Hack Firewalls appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 6th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9920, (Wed, May 6th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: New stealthy Quasar Linux malware targets software developers
items.description: A previously undocumented Linux implant named Quasar Linux (QLNX) is targeting developers' systems with a mix of rootkit, backdoor, and credential-stealing capabilities. [...]

items.title: Instructure hacker claims data theft from 8,800 schools, universities
items.description: The hacker behind a breach at education technology giant Instructure claims to have stolen 280 million data records for students and staff from 8,809 colleges, school districts, and online education platforms. [...]

items.title: Trellix Source Code Breach Highlights Growing Supply Chain Threats
items.description: Info is scant, but such breaches can reveal where a security product's controls are located and how detections are designed, giving attackers a leg up.

items.title: DAEMON Tools trojanized in supply-chain attack to deploy backdoor
items.description: Hackers trojanized installers for the DAEMON Tools software and since April 8, delivered a backdoor to thousands of systems that downloaded the product from the official website. [...]

items.title: Why Security Leadership Makes or Breaks a Pen Test
items.description: Well-run security drills go beyond checking audit boxes to identify and address trouble spots. Effective leaders can ensure proper scope, access, and follow-through, but it’s not easy.

items.title: FTC bans data broker Kochava from selling sensitive location info
items.description: The FTC has said that Kochava sold precise geolocation data showing consumers visiting houses of worship and health care clinics without their consent or awareness, an alleged violation of a law barring companies from engaging in unfair and deceptive practices.

items.title: Student hacked Taiwan high-speed rail to trigger emergency brakes
items.description: A 23-year-old university student in Taiwan was arrested for interfering with the TETRA communication system used by the country's high-speed railway network (THSR). [...]

items.title: Conti, Akira ransomware affiliate given 8-year sentence
items.description: Deniss Zolotarjovs pleaded guilty in July 2025 to money laundering and wire fraud charges after being arrested in the country of Georgia.

items.title: Critical Apache HTTP/2 Flaw (CVE-2026-23918) Enables DoS and Potential RCE
items.description: The Apache Software Foundation (ASF) has released security updates to address several security vulnerabilities in the HTTP Server, including a severe vulnerability that could potentially lead to remote code execution (RCE). The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-23918 (CVSS score: 8.8), has been described as a case of "double free and possible RCE" in the HTTP/2 protocol handling. This issue

items.title: DAEMON Tools Supply Chain Attack Compromises Official Installers with Malware
items.description: A newly identified supply chain attack targeting DAEMON Tools software has compromised its installers to serve a malicious payload, according to findings from Kaspersky. "These installers are distributed from the legitimate website of DAEMON Tools and are signed with digital certificates belonging to DAEMON Tools developers," Kaspersky researchers  Igor Kuznetsov, Georgy Kucherin, Leonid

items.title: Microsoft Edge Stores Passwords in Process Memory, Posing Enterprise Risk
items.description: A proof-of-concept exploit (PoC) shows how someone with admin privileges can exploit the issue to steal passwords, and thus use them to engage in further malicious activity.

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Sophisticated Phishing Campaign Targeting US Organizations
items.description: The malicious emails claim to contain a conduct report and lure victims to a Microsoft phishing website that leverages AitM. The post Microsoft Warns of Sophisticated Phishing Campaign Targeting US Organizations appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FTC to ban data broker Kochava from selling Americans’ location data
items.description: The FTC will ban data broker Kochava and its subsidiary, Collective Data Solutions (CDS), from selling location data without consumers' explicit consent to settle charges alleging that it sold precise geolocation data collected from hundreds of millions of mobile devices. [...]

items.title: Cleartext Passwords in MS Edge? In 2026?, (Mon, May 4th)
items.description: Yup, that is for real.

items.title: China-Linked UAT-8302 Targets Governments Using Shared APT Malware Across Regions
items.description: A sophisticated China-nexus advanced persistent threat (APT) group has been attributed to attacks targeting government entities in South America since at least late 2024 and government agencies in southeastern Europe in 2025. The activity is being tracked by Cisco Talos under the moniker UAT-8302, with post-exploitation involving the deployment of custom-made malware families that have been put

items.title: The EOL Blind Spot in Your CVE Feed: What SCA Tools Miss
items.description: Critical vulnerabilities can exist in open source software your scanners don't check. HeroDevs reveals how EOL software creates blind spots in CVE feeds and SCA tools, and how you can receive a free end-of-life scan for your projects. [...]

items.title: Hacker Conversations: Joey Melo on Hacking AI
items.description: AI red team specialist details his methods for manipulating AI guardrails through jailbreaking and data poisoning, helping developers harden machine learning models. The post Hacker Conversations: Joey Melo on Hacking AI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vimeo data breach exposes personal information of 119,000 people
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information belonging to over 119,000 people after hacking the Vimeo online video platform in April, according to data breach notification service Have I Been Pwned. [...]

items.title: Australia launches cyber review board modeled on version disbanded in US
items.description: The Cyber Incident Review Board will carry out no-fault, post-incident reviews of significant cyberattacks on Australian government and industry, focusing on systemic lessons rather than individual or corporate culpability.

items.title: Critical Bug Could Expose 300,000 Ollama Deployments to Information Theft
items.description: Dubbed Bleeding Llama, the heap out-of-bounds read issue can be exploited remotely, without authentication. The post Critical Bug Could Expose 300,000 Ollama Deployments to Information Theft appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
Critical Remote Code Execution Vulnerability Patched in Android
items.description: CVE-2026-0073 affects Android’s System component and it can be exploited without any user interaction. The post Critical Remote Code Execution Vulnerability Patched in Android appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Back Door Attackers Know About — and Most Security Teams Still Haven’t Closed
items.description: Every AI tool, workflow automation, and productivity app your employees connected to Google or Microsoft this year left something behind: a persistent OAuth token with no expiration date, no automatic cleanup, and in most organizations, no one watching it. Your perimeter controls don't see it. Your MFA doesn't stop it. And when an attacker gets hold of one, they don't need a password. OAuth

items.title: How the Story of a USB Penetration Test Went Viral
items.description: Two decades ago, pen tester Steve Stasiukonis caused a sensation by sprinkling rigged thumb drives around a credit union parking lot and following what curious employees did next. This episode looks back at the history-making event.

items.title: MetInfo CMS CVE-2026-29014 Exploited for Remote Code Execution Attacks
items.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting a critical security flaw impacting an open-source content management system (CMS) known as MetInfo, according to new findings from VulnCheck. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-29014 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in arbitrary code execution. "MetInfo CMS versions 7.9, 8.0, and 8.1 contain an unauthenticated PHP code

items.title: SSL.com rotates their root certificate today, (Tue, May 5th)
items.description: I just got an email from SSL.com last night, they are rotating &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;out their root certificate today (May 5,2026). &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;This is normal, business as usual stuff for a CA, but certificates get used for all kinds of things, and sometimes they aren&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t used like they should be, so sometimes hiccups happen.

items.title: Google now offers up to $1.5 million for some Android exploits
items.description: Google overhauls its Android and Chrome vulnerability rewards programs, offering bounties of up to $1.5 million for the most difficult exploits while scaling back payouts for flaws that artificial intelligence (AI) has made easier to find. [...]

items.title: Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities Patched in Apache MINA, HTTP Server
items.description: The most severe of these security defects could allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary code. The post Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities Patched in Apache MINA, HTTP Server appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: We Scanned 1 Million Exposed AI Services. Here's How Bad the Security Actually Is
items.description: While the software industry has made genuine strides over the past few decades to deliver products securely, the furious pace of AI adoption is putting that progress at risk. Businesses are moving fast to self-host LLM infrastructure, drawn by the promise of AI as a force multiplier and the pressure to deliver more value faster. But speed is coming at the expense of security. In the wake of the

items.title: Karakurt extortion gang ‘cold case’ negotiator gets 8.5 years in prison
items.description: A Latvian national extradited to the United States was sentenced to 8.5 years in prison for his "cold case" negotiator role in the Russian Karakurt ransomware group. [...]

items.title: ScarCruft Hacks Gaming Platform to Deploy BirdCall Malware on Android and Windows
items.description: The North Korea-aligned state-sponsored hacking group known as ScarCruft has compromised a video game platform in a supply chain espionage attack, trojanizing its components with a backdoor called BirdCallto likely target ethnic Koreans residing in China. While prior versions of the backdoor have primarily targeted Windows users only, the supply chain attack is assessed to have enabled the

items.title: German officials advance legislation that would expand law enforcement use of surveillance technology
items.description: The bills would legalize the use of automated data analysis and artificial intelligence tools that let police upload a photo of a face and scour the internet for more images depicting the same person.

items.title: Physical Cargo Theft Gets a Boost From Cybercriminals
items.description: Cargo theft is no longer about small groups of criminals operating on the ground, but transnational cybercriminal syndicates using access to supply chain systems to reroute goods.

items.title: RMM Tools Fuel Stealthy Phishing Campaign
items.description: Attackers are abusing two remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools to evade detection in a campaign that has impacted over 80 organizations so far.

items.title: Exploit Cyber-Frenzy Threatens Millions via Critical cPanel Vulnerability
items.description: Shortly after the authentication-bypass flaw was disclosed multiple proof-of-concept exploits appeared, and one researcher claims there's been zero-day activity for at least a month.

items.description: Summary

items.title: Silver Fox Springs Tax-Themed Attacks on Orgs in India, Russia
items.description: More than 1,600 socially engineered messages from the China-backed advanced persistent threat (APT) group target various sectors to deliver the previously undocumented ABCDoor backdoor, ValleyRAT, and other malware.

items.title: DShield Honeypot Update, (Mon, May 4th)
items.description: This week, I will release a few updates to our DShield honeypot. The update should happen automatically if you have "automatic updates" enabled on your system. There will be two major changes:

items.title: How Dark Reading Lifted Off the Launchpad in 2006
items.description: Twenty years ago, this media brand didn't have a print edition to attract eyeballs and sponsors. Top-notch content and editorial talent did the heavy lifting.

items.description: Wireshark release 4.6.5 fixes 43 vulnerabilities (38 CVEs) and 35 bugs.

items.title: Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
